conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. I'd like to know if I'm eligible for Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them, I need to gather some information. Let me start with a basic question:

**How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 5 years old, Blake is 17, and Charlie is 19.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Now I need to understand more about Charlie, who is 19. Child Benefit can sometimes continue past age 16 if the young person is in approved education or training.

**Is Charlie currently in full-time approved education or training (not higher education like university)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie is in full-time education - he's doing his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. A-levels at sixth form college can count as approved education.

Now I need to ask about your relationship to the children and your living situation:

**Do Alex, Blake, and Charlie live with you, and are you responsible for them?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, all three of them live with me at home and I'm responsible for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to understand your immigration status and right to claim benefits in the UK:

**Are you living in the UK, and do you have the right to claim benefits here (for example, are you a British citizen, have settled status, or have another immigration status that allows you to claim benefits)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in Manchester and I'm a British citizen, so I have the right to claim benefits here.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have a few more questions to ensure I give you an accurate assessment.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, or Charlie (for example, another parent or guardian)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to check about Charlie's education in a bit more detail, as he's 19 years old.

**When did Charlie turn 19, and is he still currently enrolled in and attending his A-level course?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He turned 19 earlier this year and yes, he's still enrolled and attending his A-level course at sixth form.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. This is important for Charlie's eligibility. 

**Do you know approximately what date Charlie turned 19, and when does his A-level course end? Also, did he start this A-level course before he turned 19?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He turned 19 in March this year. His A-level course ends in June next year, and yes, he started the course before he turned 19 - he's been doing it for about a year and a half now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that detailed information. I have one final important question:

**Do you or your partner have an individual income over £50,000 per year? This is important because of the High Income Child Benefit Charge.**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of us earns over £50,000 a year.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I've now assessed your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your children. Here's the outcome:

## Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment:

**✓ Alex (age 5) - ELIGIBLE**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. As Alex is under 16 and lives with you, and you're responsible for them, you meet all the requirements.

**✓ Blake (age 17) - ELIGIBLE**
You are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake. Blake is under 20 and lives with you, and you're responsible for them, so you meet all the requirements.

**✗ Charlie (age 19) - NOT ELIGIBLE**
Unfortunately, you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Charlie. Although Charlie is still doing A-levels at sixth form (which is approved education), Child Benefit stops on **31st August following a young person's 19th birthday**. Since Charlie turned 19 in March this year, their Child Benefit eligibility ended on 31st August this year, even though their course continues until June next year.

---

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex and Blake, but not for Charlie. Neither you nor your partner earns over £50,000, so you won't need to worry about the High Income Child Benefit Charge.

If you haven't already claimed, you should apply for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake as soon as possible, as you can only backdate claims for up to 3 months.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 5 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 5 years old, lives with the claimant, and the claimant is responsible for them. The claimant is a British citizen living in the UK with the right to claim benefits. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. As Alex is under 16 years old, they meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 17 years old, lives with the claimant, and the claimant is responsible for them. The claimant is a British citizen living in the UK with the right to claim benefits. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. As Blake is under 20 years old, they meet the age requirement for Child Benefit (the benefit can continue until age 20 if the young person is in approved education or training, though we don't need to verify this for Blake as they're only 17). Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 19 years old (turned 19 in March this year) and is currently enrolled in and attending A-level courses at sixth form college, which started before they turned 19. However, Child Benefit for young people in approved education or training only continues until the 31st August following their 19th birthday (or when they turn 20, whichever comes first). Since Charlie turned 19 in March of this year, Child Benefit eligibility would have ended on 31st August of this year, even though their A-level course continues until June next year. Therefore, the claimant is NOT currently eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is a British citizen living in the UK with three children - Alex (5), Blake (17), and Charlie (19). All children live with the claimant who is responsible for them, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for any of them. Neither the claimant nor their partner earns over £50,000, so the High Income Child Benefit Charge does not apply. The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex and Blake, but NOT for Charlie, as Child Benefit stops on 31st August following a young person's 19th birthday, even if they continue in approved education beyond that date.
